metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/api-stability.md` to the version-bump edit set: update the `'currently 0.7.1'` prose marker to `'currently 0.8.0'` (enforced by `tests/unit/test_version_sync.py` which is gated by the `make test` AC).
evidence: Plan scope lists: 'pyproject.toml', 'server.json', 'CHANGELOG.md' — docs/api-stability.md absent
docs/api-stability.md line 3: "`nava-rebar` is versioned **0.x** (currently 0.7.1)."
tests/unit/test_version_sync.py: `assert doc == pkg` where doc is the 'currently X.Y.Z' marker and pkg is pyproject.toml's version — this is an enforced CI gate
AC: '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass locally' — this test would fail without the doc update
findings.finding: docs/api-stability.md contains a hard-coded 'currently 0.7.1' prose marker that tests/unit/test_version_sync.py enforces against pyproject.toml at test time. The plan lists only three version manifests to bump (pyproject.toml, server.json, CHANGELOG.md) and omits docs/api-stability.md. If left at 0.7.1 while pyproject.toml is bumped to 0.8.0, `make test` (required by AC) will fail with a version-sync assertion error.
findings.location: What / Scope — three named edit targets; tests/unit/test_version_sync.py; docs/api-stability.md line 3
scenarios: Operator bumps pyproject.toml to 0.8.0 but misses docs/api-stability.md; `make test` fails on test_api_stability_doc_version_matches_pyproject, blocking the Gerrit Verified vote.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What / Scope' section, add a fourth bullet: `docs/api-stability.md` — update the `currently 0.7.1` prose marker to `currently 0.8.0`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a second proving command to AC3 that asserts the empty `## [Unreleased]` heading appears before `## [0.8.0]`, e.g. `awk '/## \[Unreleased\]/{u=NR} /## \[0\.8\.0\]/{v=NR} END{exit !(u>0 && v>0 && u<v)}' CHANGELOG.md`
evidence: AC3 text: 'has a `## [0.8.0] - 2026-07-16` section … AND a fresh empty `## [Unreleased]` above it'
grep command only checks for the [0.8.0] heading; the [Unreleased] heading above it is not asserted
findings.finding: AC3's proving command (`grep -n '## \[0.8.0\]' CHANGELOG.md`) only confirms the versioned heading exists but does NOT verify that a fresh empty `## [Unreleased]` section appears above it — leaving half the CHANGELOG criterion unverified by the stated command.
scenarios: A commit that promotes the section but forgets to open the new [Unreleased] heading passes AC3's stated command while the criterion's second clause is unmet.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: `awk '/## \[Unreleased\]/{u=NR} /## \[0\.8\.0\]/{v=NR} END{exit !(u>0 && v>0 && u<v)}' CHANGELOG.md`

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.
verify command `grep '^version' pyproject.toml`).` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
verify command `grep '^version' pyproject.toml` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the AC1 proving command to assert the exact value, e.g. `grep -E '^version\s*=\s*"0\.8\.0"' pyproject.toml | grep -q .` or `python -c "import tomllib; d=tomllib.load(open('pyproject.toml','rb')); assert d['project']['version']=='0.8.0'"`
evidence: 'verify: `grep '^version' pyproject.toml`' — grep exits 0 whether the line reads 0.7.1 or 0.8.0; no assertion on the captured value
Taxonomy item 6: assert on structured artifact, not prose output
findings.finding: AC1's proving command (`grep '^version' pyproject.toml`) prints matching lines but does not assert the value equals '0.8.0'; a human must inspect the output to confirm correctness, making it a display command rather than a pass/fail assertion.
scenarios: An agent running the verify command after a failed bump sees exit-0 from grep even though the version was not updated, and marks the criterion satisfied.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the grep with: `python -c "import tomllib; d=tomllib.load(open('pyproject.toml','rb')); assert d['project']['version']=='0.8.0', d['project']['version']"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a partial-failure recovery note: specify what to do if release.yml fails after PyPI publish (e.g., manually create the GitHub Release via `gh release create v0.8.0`, update MCP registry directly, or document a workflow re-run flag that skips already-completed publish steps).
evidence: Plan requires all three jobs (publish, github_release, mcp_registry) to succeed in a single run
PyPI does not allow re-uploading an already-published version — a mid-run failure leaves shared state partially created
Plan states 'approve the pypi required-reviewer gate, and monitor to completion' with no contingency for partial completion
findings.finding: The plan does not address partial-failure recovery for the release.yml workflow run: if the run fails after the PyPI publish job succeeds but before github_release or mcp_registry completes, re-dispatching the workflow will fail because PyPI rejects duplicate uploads for an existing version (0.8.0 already published). There is no stated recovery path (e.g., skip-pypi flag, manual gh release creation, or registry update) for this scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'release.yml is dispatched … publish, github_release, and mcp_registry all successful'
scenarios: release.yml run times out or network-fails after PyPI publish but before GitHub Release creation — operator cannot simply re-run the workflow
mcp_registry step fails transiently; re-dispatch re-attempts PyPI upload and fails with 'File already exists'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Failure recovery' subsection: (1) if only github_release failed, run `gh release create v0.8.0 --title 'v0.8.0' --notes-from-tag dist/*.whl dist/*.tar.gz`; (2) if only mcp_registry failed, re-trigger just that step or update the registry manually; (3) note that PyPI publish is not re-runnable for the same version so re-dispatch is only safe if publish itself did not complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan covering at minimum: (a) how to yank 0.8.0 from PyPI if a defect is found post-publish, and (b) recovery steps if the release workflow partially completes (tag exists but downstream jobs fail).
evidence: 'dispatch release.yml --ref main -f version=0.8.0, approve the pypi required-reviewer gate, and monitor to completion'
AC #6: 'publish, github_release, and mcp_registry all successful' — implies partial success states are possible
PyPI does not allow version deletion; a published 0.8.0 cannot be removed, only yanked
No mention of rollback procedure anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan describes a PyPI publish step that is irreversible (PyPI does not permit deletion of published versions, only yanking), and partially-completed release runs (e.g., tag pushed + PyPI published but MCP registry job fails) have no rollback or back-out path specified.
findings.location: What/Scope (last paragraph: 'dispatch release.yml ... approve the pypi required-reviewer gate') and AC #6
scenarios: release.yml runs: tag is created, PyPI publish succeeds, but github_release or mcp_registry job fails — 0.8.0 is live on PyPI with no GitHub Release, leaving an inconsistent state with no documented recovery path.
A bad artifact is discovered post-publish; plan provides no guidance on yanking or issuing a patch release.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'If publish succeeds but downstream jobs fail, manually complete github_release and mcp_registry steps. If a defect is discovered post-publish, run `pip install twine && twine yank nava-rebar 0.8.0` and cut a 0.8.1 patch. Do not delete the tag; annotate it as yanked.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the CHANGELOG.md scope: update the footer reference links — add `[0.8.0]: https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/compare/v0.7.1...v0.8.0` and update `[unreleased]` to `https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/compare/v0.8.0...HEAD`.
evidence: Plan says: 'retitle `## [Unreleased]` to `## [0.8.0] - 2026-07-16` and open a fresh empty `## [Unreleased]` above it' — no mention of footer links
CHANGELOG.md line 1060: `[unreleased]: https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/compare/v0.7.1...HEAD`
CHANGELOG.md line 1061: `[0.7.1]: https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/compare/v0.7.0...v0.7.1`
Keep a Changelog convention: every versioned section heading must have a corresponding comparison URL in the footer
findings.finding: The plan's CHANGELOG.md step only describes promoting the [Unreleased] header to [0.8.0] and opening a fresh [Unreleased] above it. It does not mention updating the reference-link footer at the bottom of CHANGELOG.md, which currently reads `[unreleased]: .../compare/v0.7.1...HEAD`. Keep a Changelog convention requires adding `[0.8.0]: .../compare/v0.7.1...v0.8.0` and updating `[unreleased]` to `.../compare/v0.8.0...HEAD` so the section anchors resolve correctly.
findings.location: What / Scope — CHANGELOG.md bullet; CHANGELOG.md lines 1060–1061
scenarios: Release commit lands with broken `[0.8.0]` anchor links in CHANGELOG.md — the section heading `## [0.8.0]` is a reference link but has no corresponding footer entry, so it renders as a literal string rather than a hyperlink in GitHub/PyPI rendering.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the CHANGELOG.md bullet in 'What / Scope': '…and update the footer reference links: add `[0.8.0]: .../compare/v0.7.1...v0.8.0` and retarget `[unreleased]` to `.../compare/v0.8.0...HEAD`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hardcoded `2026-07-16` in the CHANGELOG header with instruction to use the actual date the bump commit lands on `main` (or the date `release.yml` is dispatched), so the operator inserts the correct date at commit time rather than relying on a pre-planned date.
evidence: Plan specifies: 'retitle `## [Unreleased]` to `## [0.8.0] - 2026-07-16`'
AC specifies: 'CHANGELOG.md has a `## [0.8.0] - 2026-07-16` section'

The date is a planned date, not the actual landing/publication date; if the Gerrit two-vote gate or any preceding step slips to 2026-07-17 or later, the heading will be wrong in the permanently published CHANGELOG.
findings.finding: The CHANGELOG section header date `2026-07-16` is hardcoded in the plan and ACs. If the Gerrit landing or any review step slips past midnight, the published CHANGELOG will carry a misdated `## [0.8.0] - 2026-07-16` header, creating a permanent data-consistency error in the release artifact.
scenarios: Gerrit review cycle takes longer than expected, pushing the landing past midnight on 2026-07-16.
The bump commit is authored on 2026-07-16 but the dispatch and actual publication happen the next day — the CHANGELOG date predates the release.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What / Scope' CHANGELOG bullet and the corresponding AC, change `2026-07-16` to `<actual landing date>` with a note: 'set to the calendar date the bump commit lands on `main` via Gerrit (verify: `git log -1 --format=%as HEAD`)'. This makes the date a runtime-determined value rather than a fixed assumption.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the server.json scope: either update What/Scope to say 'all packages[].version' or narrow the AC verify to match 'packages[0].version', whichever reflects the actual file structure.
evidence: What/Scope: 'server.json — top-level version and packages[0].version -> 0.8.0'
AC: 'all(p[\'version\']==\'0.8.0\' for p in d[\'packages\'])' — checks ALL packages, not just index 0
findings.finding: The What/Scope section specifies updating only 'packages[0].version' in server.json, while the AC's verify script checks `all(p['version']=='0.8.0' for p in d['packages'])` — if server.json contains more than one package entry, the scope description understates the required change.
findings.location: What/Scope (server.json bullet) vs AC line 2 (server.json verify script)
scenarios: If server.json has multiple package entries, the implementer following What/Scope only updates packages[0] but the AC verify checks all of them, causing AC failure.
Conversely, if the intent is truly all packages, the What/Scope description misleads the implementer.
findings.suggested_fix: If server.json has only one package entry, change What/Scope to 'packages[].version (all entries)' to match the AC. If it truly has only one entry, note that explicitly so the AC's `all()` check is understood as correct and not over-specified.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
